Ibn Battuta: A Medieval Muslim Traveler by Ross Dunn

ideas.title: The Fatimid Caliphate: How Shia Islam Built the Most Tolerant Empire of the Medieval World
ideas.hook: The Fatimid Caliphs ruled Egypt, North Africa, and the Levant for 200 years with Christian viziers, Jewish scholars at court, and religious freedom for all — unprecedented in the medieval world.
key_facts: The Fatimids founded Cairo in 969 CE and built Al-Azhar University — today the oldest continuously operating university in the world
Under Fatimid rule, the Jewish community of Cairo produced the Cairo Geniza — 300,000 documents that are a primary source for medieval history
Caliph al-Aziz appointed a Christian as his vizier and had a Jewish court astronomer in the 990s CE
The Fatimid navy controlled Mediterranean trade routes, making Cairo wealthier than Constantinople at the dynasty's peak
sources: The Fatimids and Their Traditions of Learning by Heinz Halm
A History of Islamic Societies by Ira Lapidus

ideas.title: The Ottoman Empire's Rise: From a Tiny Principality to a Civilization That Spanned Three Continents
ideas.hook: The Ottoman Empire began as a tiny principality of 4,000 sq km in 1299. Within 200 years, it had conquered Constantinople, ended the Byzantine Empire, and governed 15 million people.
key_facts: Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II was 21 years old when he conquered Constantinople in 1453, ending 1,100 years of the Byzantine Empire
At its peak under Suleiman the Magnificent (1520-1566), the Ottoman Empire had 15 million subjects and the world's most powerful navy
The Ottoman millet system gave Christians, Jews, and Armenians legal autonomy and religious freedom under Islamic rule
The Topkapi Palace housed 4,000 staff and was the center of world governance for 400 years — longer than any equivalent institution
sources: Osman's Dream: A History of the Ottoman Empire by Caroline Finkel
The Fall of Constantinople by Steven Runciman

ideas.title: Islamic Science: The Muslim Scholars Who Invented Algebra, Optics, and Modern Medicine
ideas.hook: Every time you use the word 'algebra,' 'algorithm,' 'alcohol,' or 'zero,' you are using an Islamic invention. The scientific revolution was built on Islamic foundations Europeans spent centuries erasing.
key_facts: Al-Khwarizmi's 9th-century book 'Al-Kitab al-mukhtasar fi hisab al-jabr' gave us the words 'algebra' and 'algorithm'
Ibn al-Haytham's 'Book of Optics' (1011 CE) described how eyes form images and launched modern physics — 600 years before Newton
Al-Zahrawi (Abulcasis) wrote a 30-volume surgical encyclopedia in 1000 CE that was used as Europe's primary medical textbook until 1800
Omar Khayyam solved cubic equations geometrically and calculated the solar year to 365.24219858156 days — accurate to milliseconds
sources: Islamic Science and Engineering by Donald Hill
1001 Inventions: Muslim Heritage in Our World by Salim Al-Hassani

ideas.title: The Mamluk Sultanate: How Slave Soldiers Stopped the Mongols and Saved Islamic Civilization
ideas.hook: When the Mongols destroyed every civilization from China to Baghdad, one force stopped them: an army of soldiers who had been slaves — and their victory saved Islamic civilization from extinction.
key_facts: The Battle of Ain Jalut (1260) was the first major Mongol defeat — Mamluk slave soldiers stopped the army that had never previously lost
Mamluks were military slaves purchased as children, converted to Islam, and trained as elite cavalry — many rising to become sultans
The Mamluk Sultanate ruled Egypt and Syria for 267 years (1250-1517), preserving Islamic scholarship after Baghdad's destruction
Sultan Baybars defeated the Mongols three times and the Crusaders repeatedly, reconquering all Crusader cities except Acre by 1277
sources: Armies of Heaven by Jay Rubenstein
The Mamluks in Egyptian Politics and Society edited by Thomas Philipp

ideas.title: The Mughal Empire: How Muslim Rulers Built India's Greatest Monuments and Largest Economy
ideas.hook: In 1700, the Mughal Empire produced 25% of global GDP — more than all of Europe combined. Within 150 years, British colonialism had reduced India's share to 4%.
key_facts: At its peak under Aurangzeb (1700), the Mughal Empire governed 150 million people with a GDP that was 25% of global output
Emperor Akbar implemented a policy of religious tolerance, abolishing the jizya tax on non-Muslims and marrying Hindu princesses
The Taj Mahal required 22 years and 20,000 workers to construct; Shah Jahan allegedly planned to build an identical black marble version for himself
The Kohinoor Diamond — now in the British Crown Jewels — was taken from the Mughal treasury during British conquest of the Punjab in 1849
sources: The Mughal Empire by John F. Richards
The Anarchy: The Relentless Rise of the East India Company by William Dalrymple

ideas.title: Islamic Architecture: How Muslim Engineers Built the World's Most Advanced Structures for 1,000 Years
ideas.hook: The Hagia Sophia dome was the world's largest for 1,000 years. When Muslims conquered Constantinople, they didn't demolish it — they studied it and built bigger.
key_facts: The Great Mosque of Samarra (848 CE) had a minaret visible for 50 km and could hold 100,000 worshippers simultaneously
Persian-Islamic architects invented the muqarnas — honeycomb vaulting — that later appeared in Gothic European cathedrals
The Alhambra Palace in Granada (1238-1358) contains more mathematical symmetry per square meter than any other building ever constructed
Ottoman architect Sinan designed 374 buildings across 50 years, including the Süleymaniye Mosque whose dome exceeds the Pantheon's by 10 meters
sources: Islamic Architecture by Robert Hillenbrand
The Mosque: History, Architectural Development and Regional Diversity edited by Martin Frishman

ideas.title: The Decline of the Ottoman Empire: 200 Years of Collapse, Reform, and Western Interference
ideas.hook: For 200 years, European powers called the Ottoman Empire 'the sick man of Europe' — while simultaneously lending it money at ruinous interest rates to ensure it stayed sick.
key_facts: The Ottoman Empire borrowed £T241 million from European banks between 1854 and 1874, spending half on interest — defaulting in 1875
European powers established the Ottoman Public Debt Administration in 1881, effectively controlling the empire's finances
The Tanzimat reforms (1839-1876) attempted to modernize the empire, but European capitulations exempted foreign businesses from Ottoman law
The Armenian Genocide (1915-1923) killed between 600,000 and 1.5 million people — still denied by Turkey today
sources: The Ottoman Empire and the World Around It by Suraiya Faroqhi
A Peace to End All Peace by David Fromkin

ideas.title: The Zakat System: How Islamic Economics Designed the World's First Welfare State 1,400 Years Ago
ideas.hook: The Islamic mandatory charity system — Zakat — was designed to eliminate poverty through redistribution. Under Caliph Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz, no one could be found to receive it.
key_facts: Zakat mandates 2.5% of accumulated wealth annually be redistributed to the poor — applied to savings, not income
During Caliph Umar II's rule (717-720 CE), contemporary accounts record that no one could be found poor enough to receive Zakat in the Levant
The Islamic waqf (endowment) system funded hospitals, schools, and soup kitchens — by 1800, 75% of all productive land in the Ottoman Empire was under waqf
Modern estimates suggest if global Muslims paid full Zakat, it would generate $200-1,000 billion annually — exceeding total global development aid
sources: Economic Justice in Islam by Muhammad Baqir al-Sadr
The Economy of the Islamic State by Taqiuddin al-Nabhani

ideas.title: The Safavid Empire: How Persia Became Shia and Created the Modern Iran-Arab Divide
ideas.hook: In 1501, Shah Ismail I declared Shia Islam the state religion of Persia and forced millions to convert at sword-point. That decision created the Sunni-Shia fault line that still defines Middle East politics.
key_facts: Shah Ismail I converted Persia from majority Sunni to Shia Islam within a generation through a combination of persuasion and coercion
The Safavid-Ottoman wars (1514-1639) were the world's longest running religious and geopolitical conflict before the Cold War
Isfahan under Shah Abbas I (1588-1629) was considered the most beautiful city in the world, with 162 mosques and 1,800 caravanserais
The Safavid patronage of Persian poetry, miniature painting, and architecture created the Persian cultural identity that persists today
sources: The Safavid Empire by Andrew Newman
Empires of the Monsoon by Richard Hall

ideas.title: The First Muslims in Africa: How Islam Spread Across the Sahara and Built Empires of Gold
ideas.hook: Mansa Musa of Mali was the richest person in human history. His 1324 pilgrimage to Mecca brought so much gold to Cairo that he crashed the Egyptian economy for 10 years.
key_facts: The Mali Empire under Mansa Musa (1312-1337) controlled more than half of the world's gold and salt supply
Mansa Musa's 1324 hajj caravan of 60,000 men gave away so much gold in Cairo that it caused deflation for 10 years
Timbuktu at its peak had 180 Quranic schools and a population of 100,000 — one of medieval Africa's greatest intellectual centers
Islam spread across sub-Saharan Africa primarily through trade networks, not conquest — adopted by rulers who wanted access to the trans-Saharan economy
sources: The African Emperor by David Conrad
African History by Philip Curtin

ideas.title: The Real Story of the Crusades: Christian Holy Wars That Slaughtered Muslims, Jews, and Other Christians
ideas.hook: The First Crusaders massacred so many Muslims and Jews in Jerusalem that contemporaries described the streets running with blood up to the horses' knees. The Pope called it God's will.
key_facts: The First Crusade's capture of Jerusalem in 1099 included the massacre of approximately 30,000 Muslim and Jewish inhabitants
The Fourth Crusade (1204) never reached the Holy Land — Crusaders looted and pillaged Constantinople, a Christian city and fellow Christians
There were 9 major Crusades between 1095 and 1272, collectively killing between 1.5 and 3 million people
The Children's Crusade of 1212 sent thousands of children toward Jerusalem — most were sold into slavery by Italian merchants before leaving Europe
sources: The Crusades by Thomas Asbridge
God's War by Christopher Tyerman

ideas.title: Ibn Khaldun: The 14th-Century Muslim Thinker Who Invented Sociology, Economics, and Historiography
ideas.hook: A 14th-century Tunisian scholar wrote a theory of how civilizations rise and fall that accurately predicted the Arab Spring, the Soviet collapse, and the decline of American hegemony — 650 years in advance.
key_facts: Ibn Khaldun's Muqaddimah (1377) was the first systematic work of historical sociology, identifying cyclical patterns in civilization that modern scholars still cite
He invented the concept of 'asabiyyah' (social cohesion) as the driver of imperial rise — modern parallels include trust metrics and social capital theory
His theory predicted that empires last approximately 3-4 generations: founders create wealth, successors enjoy it, grandchildren squander it
Arnold Toynbee called the Muqaddimah 'undoubtedly the greatest work of its kind that has ever been created by any mind in any time'
sources: The Muqaddimah by Ibn Khaldun, translated by Franz Rosenthal
Ibn Khaldun: An Intellectual Biography by Robert Irwin

ideas.title: The Gulf States: How Six Small Nations Turned Desert Sand into the World's Most Ambitious Megaprojects
ideas.hook: In 1950, Dubai was a fishing village of 20,000 people. Today it has the world's tallest building, largest airport, and an economy larger than Pakistan — built in 70 years.
key_facts: The UAE was founded in 1971 from seven desert sheikhdoms; its GDP per capita ($53,000) now exceeds France, Japan, and the UK
Dubai's Palm Jumeirah added 520 km of coastline to the UAE using 94 million cubic meters of sand dredged from the sea floor
Qatar will have spent $300 billion on World Cup infrastructure — more than all previous World Cups combined
The GCC sovereign wealth funds collectively manage $4 trillion — more than the GDP of Germany
sources: Dubai: The Story of the World's Fastest City by Jim Krane
The New Silk Roads by Peter Frankopan

ideas.title: The Islamic Slave Trade: The Hidden History of 1,400 Years of Slavery Across Three Continents
ideas.hook: The Islamic slave trade lasted 1,400 years and transported an estimated 17 million Africans — more than the Atlantic slave trade. It is one of history's least-discussed atrocities.
key_facts: The Arab-run Indian Ocean slave trade began in the 7th century and continued in parts of the Arabian Peninsula until the 1960s
Historian Paul Lovejoy estimates 17 million Africans were enslaved across the trans-Saharan, Red Sea, and Indian Ocean routes over 14 centuries
Islamic law regulated but did not prohibit slavery — enslaved people had legal rights and paths to manumission not present in the Atlantic system
The Ottoman Empire used enslaved Circassians, Georgians, and Africans as household servants, soldiers, and concubines until abolition in 1882
sources: Transformations in Slavery by Paul Lovejoy
White Gold by Giles Milton

ideas.title: The Birth of Islam: The Complete Story of the Prophet Muhammad and the First Muslim Community
ideas.hook: Muhammad was illiterate, an orphan, and a merchant when he began receiving revelations in 610 CE. Within 23 years, he unified the entire Arabian Peninsula — a feat no one had accomplished in 1,000 years.
key_facts: Pre-Islamic Arabia had 360 idols in the Kaaba; Muhammad's unification abolished all of them in the 630 CE Conquest of Mecca
The Hijra (migration from Mecca to Medina in 622 CE) marks Year 1 of the Islamic calendar and was a political masterstroke that created the first Muslim state
The Constitution of Medina (622 CE) was the world's first written constitution guaranteeing rights to multiple religious communities
Within 100 years of Muhammad's death, Islam had reached Spain, West Africa, Central Asia, and the borders of China
sources: Muhammad: A Prophet for Our Time by Karen Armstrong
No God But God by Reza Aslan

ideas.title: China's Belt and Road Initiative: The $1 Trillion Infrastructure Empire That Is Rewriting the World Map
ideas.hook: China is building roads, railways, and ports in 150 countries using $1 trillion in loans. Critics call it 'debt-trap diplomacy.' Supporters call it the greatest development program in history.
key_facts: The Belt and Road Initiative (BRI), launched 2013, has signed agreements with 150 countries representing 75% of the world's population
China has committed over $1 trillion in infrastructure loans — more than the World Bank and IMF combined have deployed in the same period
Sri Lanka's Hambantota Port was leased to China for 99 years in 2017 after Sri Lanka could not repay a Chinese loan — cited as the first 'debt trap' case
The China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC) alone involves $62 billion in investment — more than Pakistan's entire annual federal budget
sources: The New Silk Roads by Peter Frankopan
China's Belt and Road: The New Geopolitics of Global Infrastructure Development by Jonathan Hillman

ideas.title: The End of American Hegemony: How the US Lost Its Unipolar Moment in 20 Years
ideas.hook: In 2000, the U.S. accounted for 31% of global GDP. By 2023, that share had fallen to 25%. Meanwhile, America fought 2 unwinnable wars, lost 7,000 soldiers, and spent $8 trillion — for nothing.
key_facts: The post-Cold War 'unipolar moment' lasted approximately 10 years; by 2008 China, Russia, and regional powers had begun challenging U.S. primacy
The Iraq and Afghanistan wars cost $8 trillion, killed 7,000 U.S. soldiers, and resulted in governments less stable than before the invasions
The 2008 financial crisis, originating in U.S. banks, permanently damaged U.S. soft power and accelerated trust in alternative financial systems
America's share of global manufacturing fell from 28% in 2000 to 16% in 2023, with China reaching 29% in the same period
sources: After the American Century by Andrew Bacevich
The Post-American World by Fareed Zakaria

ideas.title: BRICS: The New World Order Being Built Outside Western Institutions
ideas.hook: In 2024, BRICS expanded to 10 members representing 45% of the world's population and 35% of global GDP. For the first time, a bloc outside Western control is large enough to challenge the dollar system.
key_facts: BRICS (Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa) was joined by Saudi Arabia, Iran, UAE, Egypt, and Ethiopia in January 2024
The New Development Bank, BRICS' alternative to the World Bank, has approved $33 billion in infrastructure loans since 2015
BRICS nations collectively hold $4.8 trillion in foreign exchange reserves — enough to defend any member currency from speculative attacks
Over 30 additional nations have applied for BRICS membership, suggesting a structural shift away from Western-led multilateral institutions
sources: The BRICS and the Future of Global Order edited by Francis Kornegay
IMF World Economic Outlook Database 2024

ideas.title: The Sanctions Weapon: How the U.S. Uses Finance to Wage War Without Firing a Shot
ideas.hook: The U.S. has imposed sanctions on more than 9,000 individuals and entities — more than any country in history. Economists calculate that U.S. sanctions killed more Iranians than the Iran-Iraq War.
key_facts: The U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) maintains sanctions programs against 30 countries — affecting approximately 1 billion people
Cuba's sanctions, imposed in 1962, have cost the Cuban economy an estimated $130 billion over 60 years according to the UN General Assembly
Iran's sanctions regime has cut Iranian oil exports by 80%, collapsed the rial by 95%, and caused medicine shortages affecting cancer patients
The weaponization of SWIFT — excluding Russia in 2022 — accelerated 40 countries' efforts to create alternative payment systems
sources: Sanctions: The Global Company of Coercion by Francesco Giumelli
US Treasury OFAC Annual Report 2023

ideas.title: The Digital Silk Road: How China Is Building a Parallel Internet for Half the World
ideas.hook: China is building 5G networks, surveillance systems, and digital infrastructure across 70 countries. Whoever controls the infrastructure controls the data — and the data controls everything.
key_facts: Huawei has built 5G networks in 70 countries, giving China potential access to the communications infrastructure of half the developing world
China's 'Safe City' surveillance packages have been exported to 80 countries, providing AI-powered facial recognition and social monitoring systems
The U.S. 'Clean Network' initiative has convinced 53 countries to exclude Chinese telecom equipment — splitting the global internet along geopolitical lines
China's Digital Yuan (e-CNY) is designed to circumvent the dollar-dominated SWIFT system and has been tested by 260 million users domestically
sources: The Digital Silk Road by Jonathan Hillman
We Have Been Harmonised by Kai Strittmatter

ideas.title: Africa's Resource Curse: Why the Continent with 30% of Global Minerals Remains the Poorest
ideas.hook: The Democratic Republic of Congo has $24 trillion in mineral wealth — yet the average Congolese person lives on $1.77 per day. The wealth does not stay in Congo.
key_facts: Africa loses $88.6 billion annually to illicit financial flows — more than the continent receives in foreign aid and investment combined
The DRC's cobalt (70% of global supply) is extracted largely by artisanal miners, including 40,000 children, for global EV battery manufacturers
Nigeria has earned $1 trillion from oil since 1960; its Human Development Index ranking has worsened since oil was discovered
Transfer pricing — multinational companies artificially lowering African subsidiary profits — costs African governments $35 billion per year in lost taxes
sources: The Looting Machine by Tom Burgis
Africa: Why Economists Get It Wrong by Morten Jerven

ideas.title: The New Cold War: How the U.S.-China Tech War Is Splitting the World in Two
ideas.hook: The U.S. banned semiconductor exports to China in October 2022. China called it an 'act of war.' The global technology industry has been fracturing into two incompatible ecosystems ever since.
key_facts: The U.S. October 2022 chip export controls banned all advanced semiconductor equipment and personnel from reaching Chinese companies
China invested $143 billion in domestic semiconductor production between 2020 and 2025 — the largest industrial subsidy in history
ASML, the Dutch company that makes the only extreme ultraviolet lithography machines, was banned from selling to China under U.S. pressure
The bifurcation of global tech into U.S. and Chinese ecosystems could cost the global economy $1 trillion annually by 2030 according to McKinsey
sources: Chip War by Chris Miller
The Long Game by Rush Doshi

ideas.title: The Multipolar World: How the Global Power Balance Shifted Between 2001 and 2025
ideas.hook: In 2001, the U.S. was unchallenged. In 2025, China has the world's largest navy, Russia defied Western sanctions, and the Global South is demanding a new world order. The unipolar moment is over.
key_facts: The number of democracies globally peaked at 122 in 2006 and has fallen to 89 by 2023 — reversing 30 years of democratic expansion
The Global South — Africa, Latin America, Southeast Asia — collectively abstained or voted against U.S. positions at the UN 65% of the time in 2022
Saudi Arabia's joining of BRICS+ and pricing oil sales to China in yuan marked the first breach of the petrodollar system since 1974
The share of global reserves held in U.S. dollars fell from 71% in 2001 to 58% in 2023 — the fastest dedollarization in history
sources: Multipolarity and the Future of Global Order by Richard Haass, Foreign Affairs
SIPRI Yearbook 2023

ideas.title: The Opium Wars: How Britain Got China Addicted to Drugs and Ended 2,000 Years of Chinese Superiority
ideas.hook: When China tried to stop British merchants from selling opium to its citizens, Britain went to war — twice — to protect its drug dealers' profits. China calls it 'the century of humiliation.'
key_facts: By 1839, the British East India Company was selling 1,400 tonnes of opium per year to China — creating 10-12 million addicts
The First Opium War (1839-1842) ended with China ceding Hong Kong and opening 5 treaty ports to foreign trade under extraterritorial law
The Unequal Treaties forced on China after the Opium Wars are still cited by the CCP as justification for current territorial claims
China's GDP fell from 33% of global output in 1820 to 9% in 1913 — the steepest relative economic decline in history
sources: The Opium War by Julia Lovell
China: A History by John Keay

ideas.title: How Colonialism Underdeveloped Africa: The Economic Legacy of 400 Years of Extraction
ideas.hook: Walter Rodney's famous question: 'Why is Africa poor?' The answer is not geography, culture, or governance. It is a deliberate, documented process of systematic extraction that began in 1441.
key_facts: Africa exported 17 million people as slaves between 1500 and 1900 — removing the most productive demographic from its workforce
Colonial taxation systems forced Africans to work on European plantations by imposing taxes payable only in colonial currency
At independence, the Belgian Congo had only 30 African university graduates in a population of 13 million
The 'Franc Zone' system — still used by 14 African countries — requires them to deposit 50% of foreign reserves in the French Treasury in Paris
sources: How Europe Underdeveloped Africa by Walter Rodney
Africa Since 1800 by Roland Oliver and Anthony Atmore

ideas.title: The IMF and World Bank: How Western Institutions Kept Developing Nations Poor
ideas.hook: Every developing country that followed IMF 'structural adjustment' prescriptions in the 1980s and 1990s grew slower, had higher poverty, and more inequality than countries that ignored them.
key_facts: IMF structural adjustment programs in the 1980s required recipient countries to privatize state companies, cut social spending, and remove trade barriers simultaneously
A 2019 IMF internal evaluation found SAPs increased inequality in 80% of recipient countries versus comparable non-recipient nations
Bolivia's privatization of its water supply under IMF conditions led to the 2000 Cochabamba Water Wars — the price of water tripled in 3 months
The East Asian economies that defied IMF advice — South Korea, Taiwan, China — achieved the fastest growth rates in recorded economic history
sources: Globalization and Its Discontents by Joseph Stiglitz
The Shock Doctrine by Naomi Klein

ideas.title: The History of Colonialism: How Europe Divided the World and Built Its Wealth on Others' Poverty
ideas.hook: European colonialism extracted an estimated $45 trillion from India alone. Economist Utsa Patnaik's research shows Britain took enough to fund the NHS for 10,000 years.
key_facts: Economist Utsa Patnaik calculated Britain extracted $45 trillion from India between 1765 and 1938 using trade data and price records
The Berlin Conference of 1884-85 divided Africa among 14 European powers in 3 months with no African representatives present
At the start of colonialism (1492), European countries collectively produced 18% of world GDP; by 1950 that share was 50%
The 1919 Paris Peace Conference redrew borders of 3 continents in 6 months — creating most of today's conflict zones
sources: Colonialism: A Global History by Marc Frey
The Wealth of Empires by Patrick Karl O'Brien

ideas.title: The Resource Curse: Why Countries with the Most Oil and Minerals Are the Poorest
ideas.hook: Nigeria earned $1 trillion from oil since 1960 — yet more than 60% of its population lives in poverty. Venezuela had the world's largest oil reserves and descended into hyperinflation. The curse is real.
key_facts: The 'resource curse' — also called Dutch Disease — describes how natural resource wealth typically suppresses manufacturing, agriculture, and institutional development
Of 40 major oil exporting nations, 35 have per-capita income growth rates lower than oil-importing neighbors since 1970
Angola earned $500 billion in oil revenues since 1975 while 50% of its population had no access to clean water in 2020
Norway avoided the resource curse by creating a sovereign wealth fund that now holds $1.4 trillion — a model virtually no other country has replicated
sources: The Bottom Billion by Paul Collier
Blood Oil by Leif Wenar

ideas.title: The Bretton Woods System: How America Designed the Post-War Economy to Guarantee Its Own Dominance
ideas.hook: In 1944, representatives of 44 nations met in a New Hampshire hotel to design the post-war economy. The U.S. arrived with a plan that made the dollar the world's currency — permanently.
key_facts: The Bretton Woods Conference (July 1944) created the IMF, World Bank, and dollar-centered monetary system before the war had even ended
British economist John Maynard Keynes proposed a neutral global currency ('Bancor') — the U.S. delegation rejected it, insisting on dollar supremacy
The U.S. emerged from WW2 holding 75% of the world's monetary gold — giving it the leverage to impose dollar-centrism
When Nixon ended dollar-gold convertibility in 1971, he ended Bretton Woods unilaterally — without consulting the other 43 signatories
sources: The Battle of Bretton Woods by Benn Steil
Exorbitant Privilege by Barry Eichengreen

ideas.title: The Global Debt Crisis: How $307 Trillion in Debt Is Building Toward the Biggest Financial Collapse in History
ideas.hook: Global debt has reached $307 trillion — 330% of world GDP. The last time debt was this high relative to the economy was 1944. What followed was the deadliest war in human history.
key_facts: Global debt reached $307 trillion in 2023, with the U.S. alone carrying $34 trillion — exceeding its entire annual GDP
Japan's national debt is 255% of GDP — the highest ratio among advanced economies — yet it has avoided crisis through domestic bondholders
Developing countries spend more on debt interest payments than on education and healthcare combined
The Bank for International Settlements warns that $80 trillion in 'hidden' dollar debt exists through off-balance-sheet foreign exchange derivatives
sources: This Time Is Different by Reinhart and Rogoff
IIF Global Debt Monitor Q3 2023

ideas.title: The History of the Federal Reserve: How a Private Bank Controls the World Economy
ideas.hook: The Federal Reserve is not a government agency. It is a private institution owned by member banks. It has never been fully audited. And it controls the economic fate of 8 billion people.
key_facts: The Federal Reserve was created in 1913 on Jekyll Island by seven bankers representing 1/4 of the world's entire wealth — in complete secrecy
The Fed is technically owned by its member banks, which receive 6% annual dividends on their required capital contributions
Between 2008 and 2021, the Fed's balance sheet expanded from $900 billion to $8.9 trillion — purchasing assets that primarily benefited wealthy investors
The 2010 audit of the Fed's emergency lending revealed $16 trillion in secret loans to banks, foreign governments, and corporations at near-zero interest
sources: The Creature from Jekyll Island by G. Edward Griffin
Fed Up by Danielle DiMartino Booth

ideas.title: The Transatlantic Slave Trade: The Full History of History's Greatest Crime Against Humanity
ideas.hook: 12.5 million Africans were enslaved and shipped across the Atlantic. 1.8 million died on the voyage. Their labor built the wealth of four continents — and they received nothing.
key_facts: The Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database documents 36,000 slaving voyages between 1514 and 1866, transporting 12.5 million Africans
Enslaved people's unpaid labor is estimated to have generated $14 trillion in wealth for the U.S. economy between 1619 and 1865
Britain's 1807 abolition did not free enslaved people — it simply banned the trade; full emancipation came 26 years later in 1833
Haiti, the first nation founded by enslaved people who freed themselves, was forced to pay France $21 billion in reparations (in today's money) that it finished paying in 1947
sources: The Slave Ship by Marcus Rediker
The Half Has Never Been Told by Edward Baptist

ideas.title: Cryptocurrency vs. Central Banks: The War for the Future of Money
ideas.hook: Bitcoin was created specifically to replace central banks. Those same central banks are now creating their own digital currencies to eliminate Bitcoin. The war for the future of money has begun.
key_facts: The Bitcoin whitepaper was published on October 31, 2008 — 6 weeks after the Lehman Brothers collapse that triggered the global financial crisis
Bitcoin's market cap reached $1.2 trillion in 2024 — larger than the GDP of 187 of the world's 195 countries
130 countries representing 98% of global GDP are developing Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs) — programmable money that can be controlled or shut off
El Salvador became the first country to adopt Bitcoin as legal tender in 2021; the IMF threatened to withhold loans unless it reversed the policy
sources: The Bitcoin Standard by Saifedean Ammous
Atlantic Council CBDC Tracker 2024

ideas.title: The History of Inflation: Why Every Currency in History Has Eventually Become Worthless
ideas.hook: Of the 775 currencies that have existed in human history, 599 are now worthless. The average lifespan of a fiat currency is 27 years. The U.S. dollar is 53 years old without gold backing.
key_facts: Rome debased its silver denarius from 90% silver to 5% between 150-270 CE — causing inflation that preceded the empire's collapse
Weimar Germany's hyperinflation (1921-1923) peaked at 29,500% per month; workers were paid twice daily as prices doubled hourly
Zimbabwe's 2008 hyperinflation reached 89.7 sextillion percent — the central bank eventually printed $100 trillion notes
The U.S. dollar has lost 96% of its purchasing power since the Federal Reserve was created in 1913
sources: When Money Dies by Adam Fergusson
This Time Is Different by Carmen Reinhart and Kenneth Rogoff

ideas.title: The World Bank's Broken Promises: How Development Aid Made Rich Countries Richer
ideas.hook: Since 1960, the world's wealthiest nations have given $5 trillion in foreign aid to developing countries. Meanwhile, $13 trillion has flowed from developing to developed countries through debt, trade, and tax evasion.
key_facts: Global Financial Integrity estimates that $1 trillion per year leaves developing countries through illicit financial flows — dwarfing all development aid
The World Bank's own research shows that only 8% of its projects in Africa achieve their stated development goals
Aid conditionality requires recipient countries to open markets to foreign goods, destroying domestic industries before they develop
Norway, Sweden, and Denmark give 0.7% of GNI as aid as pledged by the UN in 1970; the U.S. gives 0.18% — the lowest of any OECD country
sources: Dead Aid by Dambisa Moyo
Aid and Development by Rosalind Eyben

ideas.title: The 2008 Financial Crisis: How Wall Street's Greed Almost Destroyed the Global Economy
ideas.hook: The 2008 financial crisis erased $11 trillion in household wealth in the U.S. alone, caused 10 million foreclosures, and triggered the worst global recession since 1929. Not a single banker went to prison.
key_facts: Banks packaged $2.3 trillion in subprime mortgages into securities rated AAA by agencies they were paying to rate them
The U.S. government bailout of financial institutions totaled $700 billion (TARP) plus $16 trillion in emergency Fed loans — the largest wealth transfer in history
Iceland was the only country to jail bankers responsible for the crisis — 26 executives received sentences of up to 5.5 years
The crisis erased $11 trillion in U.S. household wealth and caused 9 million job losses — all wealth recovered by 2013, largely by the top 1%
sources: The Big Short by Michael Lewis
Too Big to Fail by Andrew Ross Sorkin

ideas.title: Globalization's Winners and Losers: How Free Trade Enriched Some Countries and Destroyed Others
ideas.hook: When China joined the WTO in 2001, economists predicted benefits for all. Instead, 2 million American manufacturing jobs vanished in 10 years — while Chinese workers gained 300 million jobs. Someone won. Someone lost.
key_facts: The 'China Shock' study by economists Autor, Dorn, and Hanson showed China's WTO entry directly eliminated 2-2.4 million U.S. manufacturing jobs
Global trade grew from 20% of world GDP in 1960 to 60% in 2008 — the most rapid integration of the global economy in history
The WTO's dispute resolution mechanism has been paralyzed since 2019 when the U.S. blocked appointment of new judges to the Appellate Body
Countries that maintained industrial policy — Germany, South Korea, Japan — retained manufacturing; those that followed free-trade orthodoxy did not
sources: The China Shock by Autor, Dorn, and Hanson (American Economic Review)
Bad Samaritans by Ha-Joon Chang

ideas.title: The History of Tax Havens: How $32 Trillion Hides in Secrecy Jurisdictions While Nations Go Bankrupt
ideas.hook: Between $21 and $32 trillion is hidden in offshore tax havens — enough to end global poverty four times over. It is hidden with the full knowledge and cooperation of major Western governments.
key_facts: Tax Justice Network estimates $21-32 trillion in private wealth is hidden in offshore jurisdictions, costing governments $500 billion in lost taxes annually
The Cayman Islands — a British Overseas Territory of 65,000 people — is the registered address of 100,000 companies and 12,000 hedge funds
The Panama Papers (2016) revealed 214,000 offshore companies used by 143 politicians including 12 heads of state
Delaware, a U.S. state, offers greater corporate secrecy than most traditional offshore havens — hosting 1 million registered companies for 1 million people
sources: Treasure Islands by Nicholas Shaxson
The Hidden Wealth of Nations by Gabriel Zucman

ideas.title: The WTO and Free Trade: How Rich Countries Wrote Rules That Protect Their Own Industries
ideas.hook: Western countries demand free trade from poor nations while subsidizing their own farmers with $700 billion per year. The rules of free trade were written to benefit those who wrote them.
key_facts: OECD countries subsidize their agricultural sectors with $700 billion per year — making it impossible for African and Asian farmers to compete on price
The U.S. pays cotton farmers $4 billion per year in subsidies, collapsing world cotton prices and eliminating 10 million African livelihoods
Every major wealthy country today — the U.S., Germany, South Korea, Japan — developed behind high tariff walls before embracing free trade
Ha-Joon Chang's research shows that Britain had the world's highest import tariffs during its industrial development — 40-55% on manufactured goods
sources: Kicking Away the Ladder by Ha-Joon Chang
The WTO at 25 by Bernard Hoekman

ideas.title: The History of Microfinance: The Idea That Would End Poverty — And Why It Failed
ideas.hook: Muhammad Yunus won the Nobel Peace Prize for inventing microfinance — lending tiny amounts to the poor to start businesses. Two decades later, the evidence shows it rarely works. Why?
key_facts: Muhammad Yunus founded Grameen Bank in 1983; by 2010 it had 8.4 million borrowers and the microfinance industry had grown to $100 billion globally
A 2015 MIT meta-study of 6 randomized controlled trials found microfinance had no measurable impact on poverty reduction or business growth
In Andhra Pradesh, India, a 2010 microfinance crisis saw 30 borrowers commit suicide after aggressive loan collection practices
Empirical evidence consistently shows that grants — not loans — are the most effective poverty intervention, but they generate no profit for lenders
sources: A Fistful of Rice by Vikram Akula
Poor Economics by Abhijit Banerjee and Esther Duflo

ideas.title: The Green Economy: How the Climate Crisis Is Creating the Largest Economic Opportunity in History
ideas.hook: The transition to a clean energy economy requires $150 trillion in investment by 2050. Every dollar invested in green infrastructure generates $4 in economic returns. The question is who captures the gains.
key_facts: Solar energy is now the cheapest source of electricity in human history — 90% cheaper than in 2010 — yet fossil fuel subsidies still total $7 trillion globally per year
China manufactures 80% of global solar panels, 60% of wind turbines, and 75% of lithium-ion batteries — dominating the clean energy supply chain
The IEA estimates the clean energy transition requires $150 trillion by 2050 — the largest investment in any single sector in history
Lithium, cobalt, and rare earth elements required for the energy transition are concentrated in the Global South — creating new resource dependencies
sources: The New Map by Daniel Yergin
IEA World Energy Outlook 2023

ideas.title: The History of Inequality: How the Gap Between Rich and Poor Has Grown for 200 Years — And How to Close It
ideas.hook: In 1820, the richest 10% earned 3 times more than the poorest 10%. Today they earn 40 times more. The gap has never been wider — and it is accelerating.
key_facts: The world's 2,640 billionaires gained $3.3 trillion in wealth between 2020 and 2022 — more than the combined wealth of the bottom 4.6 billion people
Thomas Piketty's r > g formula shows that returns on capital consistently outpace economic growth, mathematically guaranteeing increasing inequality
The post-WW2 period (1945-1980) saw inequality fall in virtually every country due to high taxes, strong unions, and capital controls
Oxfam calculates that a 2% wealth tax on the world's billionaires would raise $2.52 trillion — enough to lift 2 billion people out of poverty
sources: Capital in the 21st Century by Thomas Piketty
The Spirit Level by Richard Wilkinson and Kate Pickett
